children.name: Mus
children.description: The house mouse and its relatives — small, opportunistic omnivores that have followed human civilisation to every continent. Mus musculus is one of the most studied vertebrates on Earth: its genome was sequenced in 2002, and laboratory mice underpin the majority of biomedical research. Wild house mice are extraordinarily adaptable, surviving on Antarctic research stations and in the refrigerated meat lockers of supermarkets at near-freezing temperatures. Their incisors grow continuously and must be worn down by gnawing.

children.name: Mus musculus
children.description: The most abundant mammal on Earth by number of individuals. Domesticated as the 'lab mouse', it has contributed to more Nobel Prizes than any other animal.

children.name: Mus spretus
children.description: A wild relative of the house mouse found around the western Mediterranean. Resistant to certain pesticides that affect house mice, making it a subject of genetic research.

children.name: Mus caroli
children.description: Native to island Southeast Asia and southern China, now also established on Taiwan, the Ryukyu Islands, and parts of Japan through commensal spread. Genetically important for understanding the divergence of the Mus subgenus. Smaller than the house mouse with a proportionally longer tail.

children.name: Mus pahari
children.description: An unusual Mus with a notably elongated snout, inhabiting montane forests from northeast India through Myanmar to southern China and Vietnam. It forages in leaf litter and under bark for insects as well as seeds, a more insectivorous diet than most Mus. The species is used in comparative genomics due to its divergence from the common house mouse lineage.

children.name: Mus cervicolor
children.description: A pale, deer-coloured mouse of open habitats and rice paddies across South and Southeast Asia. It is a minor agricultural pest in some areas and an important food source for barn owls and small raptors. Populations on offshore islands are sometimes treated as separate subspecies.

children.name: Mus booduga
children.description: One of the smallest members of the genus, widespread in grasslands and cultivated fields of the Indian subcontinent and Sri Lanka. Despite its tiny size it can be locally common and is an important component of owl pellets across much of India. Tolerates the dry Deccan Plateau conditions that exclude many other small mammals.

children.name: Mus shortridgei
children.description: A rare mouse of seasonally dry grasslands in Myanmar, Thailand, and Cambodia, associated with tall grass near water. IUCN-listed as Near Threatened due to habitat loss from agriculture and grassland burning. Named after the British zoologist Guy Shortridge who made major contributions to South Asian mammalogy.

children.name: Mus terricolor
children.description: A small mouse from South Asian farmlands; major pest of stored grain.

children.name: Mus nitidulus
children.description: A Southeast Asian mouse with glossy coat; occupies dry deciduous forests.

children.name: Mus fragilicauda
children.description: A Thai species with tail that autotomises extremely easily as predator escape.

children.name: Mus majori
children.description: An Indonesian endemic from Sumatra and Java montane forests.

children.name: Rattus
children.description: Rats — medium-sized, highly intelligent rodents with a deeply troubled relationship with humanity. The black rat (Rattus rattus) was the reservoir host for the fleas that spread Yersinia pestis, causing the Black Death. The brown rat (Rattus norvegicus), despite its name, originated in central Asia and reached Europe in the 18th century, largely displacing the black rat. Both species have been implicated in the extinction of numerous island birds and reptiles. Rats also demonstrate empathy: laboratory brown rats will forgo food rewards to release trapped companions.

children.name: Rattus norvegicus
children.description: The dominant commensal rat across most of the world. Lab rats are domesticated brown rats. Wild populations can learn to avoid novel objects (neophobia) and pass this learned behaviour to offspring.

children.name: Rattus rattus
children.description: A superb climber and the original ship rat — spread worldwide by European maritime trade. Responsible for devastating seabird colonies on remote islands; eradication programs have allowed remarkable recoveries.

children.name: Rattus exulans
children.description: The third most widely distributed rat in the world, spread throughout the Pacific by Polynesian voyagers as a storied commensal and food source. Archaeological evidence of its presence is used to track human migration across the Pacific. Smaller than the brown or black rat, it is more agile in dense vegetation.

children.name: Rattus tanezumi
children.description: Long confused with Rattus rattus and only separated as a full species by molecular studies, the Oriental house rat is the dominant commensal rat across much of eastern and Southeast Asia. It competes with R. rattus wherever their ranges overlap, frequently excluding the black rat in urban areas. An important reservoir host for several zoonotic pathogens in Asia.

children.name: Rattus praetor
children.description: A large, coarse-furred rat of rainforest and secondary growth across New Guinea and the Solomon Islands. One of the heftiest members of the genus, reaching nearly 300 g. It plays an important ecological role as a seed predator and prey item for large owls and pythons in Melanesian rainforests.

children.name: Rattus sordidus
children.description: A significant agricultural pest of sugarcane and grain crops in northern Australia and New Guinea. Populations undergo dramatic boom-and-bust cycles tied to rainfall and food availability. In peak years canefield rat numbers can be extraordinarily high, causing millions of dollars of crop damage.

children.name: Rattus fuscipes
children.description: Australia's most widespread native rat, inhabiting heathlands, dry sclerophyll forest, and rainforest fringes along the eastern and southwestern coasts. Unlike invasive rats it is a shy forest dweller that avoids human settlement. Bush rats declined sharply after fox and cat introductions but have recovered in areas with predator control.

children.name: Rattus villosissimus
children.description: An irruptive Australian native rat of arid and semi-arid inland Australia that undergoes spectacular population explosions after heavy rainfall. Densities can reach hundreds per hectare during a plague year, driving surges in predator populations of dingoes, raptors, and snakes. Population crashes are equally rapid as the food supply is exhausted.

children.name: Rattus leucopus
children.description: A native Australian rat from Cape York and southern New Guinea with white feet.

children.name: Apodemus
children.description: Wood mice and field mice of the Old World — elegant, large-eared mice that inhabit woodland, hedgerow, and field edges across Europe and Asia. The wood mouse (Apodemus sylvaticus) is one of the most abundant wild mammals in Europe and a crucial prey species for owls, foxes, stoats, and weasels. It caches seeds and has a remarkable ability to locate buried food stores it has hidden months earlier. The yellow-necked mouse is larger and bolder, readily entering houses in autumn.

children.name: Apodemus sylvaticus
children.description: One of Europe's most abundant small mammals, forming the base of countless food chains. Can shed its tail skin when seized by a predator, leaving the predator holding only skin while the mouse escapes.

children.name: Apodemus flavicollis
children.description: Identified by its distinctive yellow collar. Larger and more arboreal than the wood mouse, readily climbing high into trees. An important seed disperser in European deciduous woodland.

children.name: Apodemus uralensis
children.description: The smallest Apodemus in Europe and western Asia, occupying grasslands, scrub, and forest edges from the Balkans east to the Urals and beyond. Molecularly distinct from A. sylvaticus but long confused with it morphologically. An important prey species in open farmland habitats.

children.name: Apodemus agrarius
children.description: Immediately recognised by the dark dorsal stripe running from head to tail — rare in European murids. Highly associated with agricultural land and forest edges, it is often the most abundant small mammal in cultivated landscapes from central Europe to China. A reservoir host for Hantavirus (Dobrava strain) in parts of eastern Europe.

children.name: Apodemus mystacinus
children.description: A large, robust Apodemus of rocky limestone terrain and scrub-covered hillsides around the eastern Mediterranean, extending through Turkey into the Middle East. Adapted to arid, stony habitats where other Apodemus are scarce. Its relatively broad molars suggest a harder, more varied diet than its congeners.

children.name: Apodemus peninsulae
children.description: A boreal-zone species ranging across the forests of Korea, Manchuria, Siberia, and into Mongolia. One of the most northerly Apodemus, active year-round without hibernation by storing large fat reserves and food caches. A known reservoir of Seoul Hantavirus and related strains in northeastern Asia.

children.name: Mastomys
children.description: Multimammate rats — African rodents named for their extraordinary number of mammary glands (up to 24, versus the usual 10–12 in most rodents). They are among the most prolific breeders of any mammal, capable of producing litters of up to 22 young multiple times per year. Mastomys natalensis is the primary reservoir for Lassa fever virus in West Africa, a viral haemorrhagic fever that infects 100,000–300,000 people annually. Occasional population explosions create rodent plagues that devastate crops across sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Mastomys natalensis
children.description: The most abundant rodent in sub-Saharan Africa and one of the most fecund mammals on Earth. Reservoir host of Lassa virus, which it sheds asymptomatically in urine and faeces.

children.name: Mastomys coucha
children.description: Long confused with M. natalensis, the southern multimammate mouse is now recognised as a distinct karyotypically separate species confined to southern Africa. It inhabits open grassland, Karoo scrub, and fallow farmland. A laboratory colony established in the 1960s has contributed to cancer and parasite research.

children.name: Mastomys erythroleucus
children.description: A West African savanna species associated with Guinea woodland and forest-savanna mosaic habitats. Like its relatives it undergoes periodic population explosions after good rains, briefly becoming a pest of stored grain. Karyotypic and molecular data clearly distinguish it from the more widespread M. natalensis.

children.name: Praomys
children.description: Soft-furred rats — a genus of small to medium-sized African murids found primarily in forest and forest-edge habitats across sub-Saharan Africa. Praomys species are often indicators of intact forest health and are among the first to disappear when forest is degraded. They are insectivorous-omnivores, feeding on invertebrates, fungi, and fruit. Several species are restricted to montane forest isolates and are threatened by deforestation.

children.name: Praomys tullbergi
children.description: A widespread West and Central African forest-floor rodent found from Sierra Leone to the Congo Basin. Often the most abundant small mammal in undisturbed lowland rainforest. Named after the Swedish zoologist Tycho Tullberg, an authority on rodent anatomy.

children.name: Praomys jacksoni
children.description: Distributed across the montane forests of East and Central Africa, including the Albertine Rift highlands. It is among the commoner small mammals in Ugandan and Rwandan forest reserves. Named for the British administrator and natural historian Sir Frederick Jackson, whose collections advanced knowledge of East African fauna.

children.name: Praomys misonnei
children.description: A forest specialist of the Congo Basin known from relatively few specimens. Named after the Belgian mammalogist Xavier Misonne who made major contributions to African rodent taxonomy. IUCN lists it as Data Deficient due to the scarcity of survey data from its range.

children.name: Praomys dekeri
children.description: A South African species from riverine forests with exceptionally soft pelage.

children.name: Aethomys
children.description: Rock rats and bush rats of sub-Saharan Africa — medium-sized murids with coarser fur than Praomys, typically associated with rocky outcrops, boulder fields, and dry savanna. Aethomys species are adept climbers and often nest in rock crevices or under the bark of large trees. They are important prey items for African rock pythons, eagle owls, and large mongooses. The genus likely radiates from a southern African ancestor, with species diversifying along the eastern savanna belt.

children.name: Aethomys chrysophilus
children.description: The most widespread Aethomys, ranging from Tanzania south to South Africa in rocky savanna and woodland. Its warm rufous coat gives it its common name. Seed-eater and frugivore, it plays a role in dispersing figs and other large-seeded trees.

children.name: Aethomys hindei
children.description: Restricted to rocky hillsides and inselbergs in Kenya and Tanzania. Named after the British military officer and naturalist Sidney Hinde who collected mammals in East Africa in the 1890s. A poorly known species with few records, likely more widespread in suitable rocky habitat.

children.name: Aethomys kaiseri
children.description: A Central African species occurring in miombo woodland and rocky areas of the Congo Basin periphery and Zambia. Named for a German colonial administrator who facilitated zoological collecting in the region. Its ecology is poorly studied but it appears to have a broad omnivorous diet.

children.name: Aethomys namaquensis
children.description: A rock-dwelling species from arid southwestern Africa; stores seeds in deep crevices.

children.name: Lemniscomys
children.description: Striped grass mice — among the most visually distinctive African murids, most species bearing bold longitudinal stripes or spots on a tawny background that provide disruptive camouflage in dry grassland. They are diurnal and crepuscular, highly active in open grass and light scrub, making them unusual among murids which are mostly nocturnal. Lemniscomys species are important prey for raptors, large lizards, and small carnivores in African savanna ecosystems. Several species co-occur on the same grasslands, apparently partitioning resources by grass layer and seed size.

children.name: Lemniscomys striatus
children.description: The most widespread striped grass mouse, ranging across the forest-savanna mosaic zone of West and Central Africa. Its many fine white dorsal stripes distinguish it from the spotted-and-striped L. bellieri. An energetic diurnal forager and a favoured prey item of secretary birds and African marsh owls.

children.name: Lemniscomys barbarus
children.description: The only striped grass mouse north of the Sahara, restricted to coastal northwestern Africa from Morocco to Tunisia. Inhabits scrub, rough pasture, and cereal fields. It was one of the first African rodents described to European science, collected during the Barbary Coast expeditions of the 17th century.

children.name: Lemniscomys griselda
children.description: A southern African species associated with miombo woodland and Zambezian grassland from Angola and Zambia south to Zimbabwe. Paler and more sparsely striped than northern relatives. Like other Lemniscomys it builds a grass-lined nest in a shallow burrow or under a termite mound.

children.name: Lophuromys
children.description: Harsh-furred mice — a genus of stiff-pelaged, semi-insectivorous murids confined to Afromontane and Guinean forests. The coarse, almost bristly fur is a hallmark of the genus. Most species are associated with moist montane grassland, bamboo thicket, and the understorey of highland forests. Lophuromys species are unusual murids in that invertebrates — beetles, earthworms, and larvae — may form the majority of the diet. Several species are found only on single mountain massifs, making them highly vulnerable to habitat loss.

children.name: Lophuromys sikapusi
children.description: The most widely distributed harsh-furred mouse, ranging across the moist forest belt from Sierra Leone to western Kenya. It occupies a broad elevational range from lowland forest to montane grassland at 3,000 m. The rufous underside is distinctive; captive individuals have revealed a remarkably insectivorous diet for a murid.

children.name: Lophuromys flavopunctatus
children.description: An East African highland species abundant on the Ethiopian Plateau, Kenyan highlands, and the Albertine Rift, where it can be the most common small mammal in montane heath and afroalpine grassland above 2,500 m. Yellow-speckled fur gives it its species name. An important prey item for Ethiopian wolves foraging in Afroalpine habitats.

children.name: Lophuromys aquilus
children.description: Restricted to the montane forests and bamboo zones of Kenya's central highlands and the Ugandan side of Mount Elgon. A darker, more uniform species than L. flavopunctatus. Poorly studied; most records come from owl pellets and pitfall traps set by researchers targeting other taxa.

children.description: A recently described species from the Bale Mountains of Ethiopia.

children.name: Maxomys
children.description: Spiny rats of Southeast Asia — a diverse genus of medium to large murids widespread across Sundaland, the Philippines, and Sulawesi, predominantly in primary and mature secondary lowland and montane rainforest. Most Maxomys species have distinctly spiny dorsal pelage providing protection against predators. They are important frugivores and seed predators in Asian rainforests, particularly of dipterocarp-family trees. The genus has diversified extensively across the islands of Southeast Asia, with many island-endemic species.

children.name: Maxomys surifer
children.description: The most widespread Maxomys, ranging from southern Thailand and Myanmar through the Malay Peninsula to Borneo and Sumatra. Its strikingly rufous upperparts make it one of the most recognisable rainforest rodents in Sundaland. It forages on the forest floor for fallen seeds, fruit, and invertebrates and is an important prey item for Burmese pythons and clouded leopards.

children.name: Maxomys rajah
children.description: A large, heavily built spiny rat of Borneo and the Thai-Malay peninsula, associated with primary lowland dipterocarp forest and riverine forest. Less commonly trapped than M. surifer and apparently requiring larger intact forest blocks. Its role as a seed predator of commercially important timber trees has been studied in Sabah.

children.name: Maxomys whiteheadi
children.description: A small Maxomys of Borneo, Sumatra, and the Riau Archipelago, inhabiting both primary forest and well-grown secondary forest. Named after John Whitehead, the British ornithologist and naturalist who made major collections in Borneo and the Philippines in the 1880s to 1890s. One of the more tolerant spiny rats of forest edge habitat.

children.name: Gerbillus
children.description: Dwarf gerbils — the most species-rich gerbil genus, with over 40 recognised species spanning arid and hyper-arid zones from West Africa across the Sahara to Pakistan. Most Gerbillus are tiny, weighing only 10–30 g, with disproportionately large eyes and ears suited to nocturnal life in open desert. Their kidneys are exceptionally efficient; several species can survive indefinitely on dry seeds without drinking water. They excavate extensive shallow burrow systems and are primary prey for desert snakes, owls, and small carnivores.

children.name: Gerbillus pyramidum
children.description: One of the larger Gerbillus, found in sandy desert from Egypt and Libya east to Israel and the Sinai. Associated with sandy substrates where it excavates burrow systems at the base of tussock grasses. The common name references the species' historical abundance in the vicinity of the Egyptian pyramids.

children.name: Gerbillus campestris
children.description: Widely distributed across North Africa from Mauritania to Egypt in stony semi-desert and open scrub. Unlike most Gerbillus it tolerates harder substrates and is less restricted to pure sand. An important prey species for fennec foxes and Saharan eagle owls throughout its range.

children.name: Gerbillus gerbillus
children.description: The type species of the genus, inhabiting sandy desert dunes and sandy plains from Morocco to the Arabian Peninsula. A highly efficient water-conserving rodent that almost never needs to drink. Nocturnal and solitary outside the breeding season, it uses saltatory locomotion to move rapidly across open sand.

children.name: Gerbillus nanus
children.description: A small, pale gerbil of rocky semi-desert from North Africa through the Arabian Peninsula to Pakistan and northwestern India. More tolerant of rocky and gravelly terrain than many congeners. Its range spans some of the world's most hyper-arid environments where it serves as a key link in desert food chains.

children.name: Gerbillus andersoni
children.description: A coastal gerbil from Libya to Sinai; pale sandy fur conceals it in dunes.

children.name: Meriones
children.description: Gerbils — social, burrowing rodents of arid and semi-arid regions from North Africa to China. The Mongolian gerbil became a popular laboratory animal and pet in the 1950s; all captive gerbils descend from just 20 individuals caught in Mongolia in 1935. Wild gerbils live in complex multi-chamber burrow systems and are active both day and night. Their kidneys are extraordinarily efficient at concentrating urine — some species can survive on dry seeds alone, producing almost no liquid waste.

children.name: Meriones unguiculatus
children.description: The standard laboratory gerbil. Highly social — solitary individuals develop stereotypic behaviours and show signs of depression. They mark territories with a scent gland on their abdomen.

children.name: Meriones libycus
children.description: A large gerbil of North Africa and the Middle East. Can be a significant agricultural pest, storing large quantities of grain in its burrows. Important prey for desert foxes, eagles, and snakes.

children.name: Meriones meridianus
children.description: A Central Asian species ranging from the Caspian lowlands east to Mongolia and northern China, inhabiting sandy and loamy semi-desert steppes. The name alludes to its crepuscular and midday activity pattern, unusual among gerbils. It forms loose social colonies and stores large seed caches for winter.

children.name: Meriones persicus
children.description: A large, powerfully built jird of rocky slopes and foothill steppes in Iran, Turkey, and the South Caucasus. Strictly nocturnal, it excavates deep complex burrows among boulders. The Persian jird has been used as a laboratory model for studying Eimeria coccidian parasites.

children.name: Meriones tristrami
children.description: A medium-sized jird of the eastern Mediterranean and Middle East, ranging from Turkey and Israel to Iraq and Sinai. Named after the Victorian naturalist Henry Baker Tristram, a prolific collector in the Holy Land. It inhabits cultivated fields, sand dunes, and the margins of irrigated land.

children.name: Meriones crassus
children.description: A stocky jird of Middle Eastern deserts; stores large seed caches underground.

children.name: Taterillus
children.description: Savanna gerbils of sub-Saharan Africa — slender, long-tailed gerbils with large ears that occupy a similar ecological niche in African savannas to Gerbillus in the Sahara. Most species are found in the Sahel and Sudan savanna zones where they dig burrow systems in sandy soils. The taxonomy of the genus is complicated by cryptic species that are morphologically identical but karyotypically distinct. They are primarily granivorous and are important prey for small raptors and serval cats.

children.name: Taterillus gracilis
children.description: Distributed across the Sahel belt from Senegal to Chad in sandy savanna with sparse grass. One of the more gracile savanna gerbils, with a notably long tail relative to body length. Karyotypic studies have revealed cryptic diversity within what was long considered a single species.

children.name: Taterillus emini
children.description: A savanna gerbil of the Sudan-Guinea zone from Cameroon east to South Sudan and Uganda, inhabiting sandy soil in open woodland and tall-grass savanna. Named after Emin Pasha (Eduard Schnitzer), the German-Ottoman governor and naturalist who made extensive natural history collections in Central Africa. Like other Taterillus it is karyotypically complex.

children.name: Grammomys
children.description: Woodland thicket rats — an African genus of slender, long-tailed murids distinguished by their arboreal and semi-arboreal habits, in contrast to most African rodents which are terrestrial. Grammomys species use their long tails for balance when moving through dense tangles of vegetation and lianas in forest edge and thicket. They are omnivores feeding on fruit, seeds, and insects. Several species co-occur in East African forests with overlapping ranges but use different strata.

children.name: Grammomys dolichurus
children.description: The most widespread Grammomys, ranging from West Africa to South Africa in forest-edge, thicket, and riverine bush. It builds a spherical nest of leaves and grass suspended in thorny vegetation. An agile climber, it is one of the few African murids regularly found more than 2 m above ground.

children.name: Grammomys surdaster

children.name: Hylomyscus
children.description: African wood mice — a genus of small, large-eared forest murids superficially resembling the European Apodemus but independently adapted to life on the Afrotropical forest floor and lower canopy. Hylomyscus species have soft, velvety fur and exceptionally large eyes relative to their body size, adaptations for life in the dim rainforest understorey. They are seed-eaters and frugivores, with some species taking considerable quantities of arthropods. Many species are restricted to a single montane forest block and are of conservation concern.

children.name: Hylomyscus alleni
children.description: Widespread in lowland rainforest from Nigeria to the western Congo Basin, Allen's wood mouse is one of the commoner Hylomyscus in West African forest. Named after Joel Asaph Allen, the American mammalogist and ornithologist who described many African small mammals from museum collections in the late 19th century.

children.name: Hylomyscus stella
children.description: A montane forest specialist of the Albertine Rift highlands, Mount Cameroon, and the Bioko Island forests. It occupies the leaf-litter layer of dense montane forest between 1,500 and 3,000 m elevation. Often caught alongside Lophuromys species in pitfall arrays, suggesting it occupies a similar niche at higher elevations.

children.name: Hylomyscus anselli
children.description: A recently described Hylomyscus from the montane forests of Zambia and Malawi, associated with the Nyika Plateau and Mulanje Massif. Named after the British mammalogist William Frank Herbert Ansell, whose work on Zambian mammals remains a foundational reference. Considered Near Threatened due to the restricted extent of its Afromontane forest habitat.

children.name: Arvicanthis
children.description: African grass rats — medium-sized, diurnal murids of open grassland and cultivated land across sub-Saharan Africa and the Nile Valley. Arvicanthis species are exceptional among rodents in being predominantly diurnal, active in full sunlight. They are model organisms for circadian rhythm research, as their daily activity cycle is opposite to that of standard lab rodents. Arvicanthis niloticus and relatives can reach enormous densities in agricultural areas, causing serious crop damage during population irruptions.

children.name: Arvicanthis niloticus
children.description: A major agricultural pest of the Nile Valley and sub-Saharan savanna. Its diurnal activity has made it an important model organism for studying mammalian circadian clocks in the context of natural light-dark cycles, as its clock mechanisms differ fundamentally from those of nocturnal mice.

children.name: Arvicanthis somalicus
children.description: A species of the arid Horn of Africa, inhabiting stony semi-desert and dry grassland in Somalia, Ethiopia, and Kenya. More drought-tolerant than A. niloticus, it can persist in areas with very low annual rainfall. An important component of the diet of martial eagles and pale chanting goshawks in the Somali-Masai biome.

children.name: Rhabdomys
children.description: Striped mice of southern Africa — small, elegantly striped murids endemic to sub-Saharan Africa, particularly the south and east. Rhabdomys species resemble Lemniscomys in their striped pattern but belong to a quite separate lineage. They are diurnal in open habitats and partly nocturnal in denser vegetation. Two to four cryptic species have been distinguished within what was long considered a single species (R. pumilio), with different ecological tolerances. They are important prey for small raptors and mongooses and help regulate invertebrate populations through foraging.

children.name: Rhabdomys pumilio
children.description: One of the most abundant small mammals in southern African fynbos, Karoo, and grassland. Four bold dark stripes on the back provide camouflage in dry grass. Recent molecular studies show this 'species' is actually a species complex; populations in arid and mesic zones are genetically distinct.

children.name: Rhabdomys dilectus
children.description: Segregated from R. pumilio by molecular analysis, this species occupies mesic grassland and forest margins in eastern South Africa, Lesotho, Swaziland, and Zimbabwe. Slightly darker than R. pumilio and associated with higher rainfall zones. It has been used in studies of social behaviour and group living in African murids.
